title: Speaking Machine Quiz
description: Test your mastery of the Linux terminal and commands.

questions.question: Which command is used to display the current directory you are working in?
questions.explanation: pwd (Print Working Directory) shows your current location in the file system.

questions.question: The 'rm' command moves files to a 'Recycle Bin' for later retrieval.
questions.explanation: False. In the terminal, 'rm' deletes files immediately and permanently.

questions.question: To create a new, empty directory, you use the ______ command.
questions.explanation: mkdir stands for Make Directory.

questions.question: What does 'sudo' stand for?
questions.explanation: sudo allows a user to execute commands with the privileges of the superuser (root).

questions.question: You see a file with permissions '-rwxr--r--'. What can the 'Owner' do that others cannot?
options: Write to and Execute the file
questions.explanation: The first triplet 'rwx' is for the owner. Others have 'r--' (read only).

questions.question: The ______ symbol is used to send the output of one command to the input of another.
questions.explanation: The pipe symbol '|' is used for command chaining.

questions.question: Which command is used to search for a specific pattern of text in a file?
questions.explanation: grep (Global Regular Expression Print) is the standard text searching tool.

questions.question: Linux is 'Open Source', meaning its source code is freely available for anyone to study and modify.
questions.explanation: This transparent nature is why many security tools are built on Linux.

questions.question: What is a 'Distro'?
options: A specific version of the Linux OS (e.g., Kali, Ubuntu)
A hardware component in a router
A command to delete a directory
questions.explanation: Distro is short for Distribution, which is a specific version of Linux.

questions.question: How do you move 'back' one level in the folder structure?
questions.explanation: 'cd ..' is the universal command to move to the parent directory.

questions.question: The first line of a Bash script, `#! /bin/bash`, is called the ______.
questions.explanation: The shebang tells the OS which interpreter to use to run the script.

questions.question: Which of these commands would create a file named 'notes.txt'?
questions.explanation: touch creates a file; mkdir creates a folder.

questions.question: What happens if you run 'cat large_file.txt'?
options: It prints the entire content of the file to the terminal
questions.explanation: cat outputs the file content directly to the screen.

questions.question: In Linux, everything is considered a file, including your keyboard and your mouse.
questions.explanation: This is a core philosophy of Unix-like systems: 'Everything is a file'.

questions.question: To give a file 'Execute' permission, you use:
questions.explanation: '+x' adds the execute permission.

questions.question: The root directory in Linux is represented by the ______ symbol.
questions.explanation: The forward slash '/' is the root of the entire file system.

questions.question: Which command would show hidden files in a directory?
questions.explanation: '-a' stands for all, including hidden files (those starting with a dot).

questions.question: Why would you use 'ls -l' instead of just 'ls'?
options: To see files in large font
To see a 'long' listing with details like permissions and size
questions.explanation: '-l' provides the long-format output with metadata.

questions.question: You should always use 'sudo' for every command you type, even if it doesn't need admin power.
questions.explanation: False. Only use sudo when necessary. It's dangerous to run everything as root!

questions.question: What is the command to create a script and then run it?
questions.explanation: Create, make executable, and then execute.
